rule:vanthoff.school.temperature_rate.name: Effect of temperature on reaction rate
rule:vanthoff.school.temperature_rate.short_statement: For every 10 K rise in temperature, the reaction rate increases 2–4 times.
rule:vanthoff.school.temperature_rate.explanation: School formulation of Van't Hoff's rule. Heating the reaction mixture by 10°C roughly doubles to quadruples the rate of a homogeneous reaction, because more molecules acquire sufficient energy to overcome the activation barrier.

rule:kinetics.concentration.rate.name: Effect of concentration on reaction rate
rule:kinetics.concentration.rate.short_statement: Increasing the concentration of reactants increases the reaction rate.
rule:kinetics.concentration.rate.explanation: Law of mass action: the rate of a chemical reaction is directly proportional to the concentrations of the reacting substances. Higher concentrations mean more frequent collisions between reactant molecules.

rule:kinetics.surface_area.rate.name: Effect of surface area on reaction rate
rule:kinetics.surface_area.rate.short_statement: Increasing the surface area of reactants increases the rate of a heterogeneous reaction.
rule:kinetics.surface_area.rate.explanation: Grinding solid reactants and using porous materials increases the contact area between phases. Since a heterogeneous reaction occurs at the phase boundary, surface area determines the number of active sites.

rule:kinetics.catalyst.activation_energy.name: Effect of catalyst on activation energy
rule:kinetics.catalyst.activation_energy.short_statement: A catalyst lowers the activation energy of a reaction.
rule:kinetics.catalyst.activation_energy.explanation: A catalyst changes the reaction mechanism by providing an alternative pathway with lower-energy intermediates, reducing the overall energy barrier of the reaction.

rule:kinetics.catalyst.rate.name: Effect of catalyst on reaction rate
rule:kinetics.catalyst.rate.short_statement: A catalyst speeds up a chemical reaction without being consumed.
rule:kinetics.catalyst.rate.explanation: By lowering the activation energy, a larger fraction of molecules can overcome the energy barrier at the same temperature, increasing the reaction rate. The catalyst is regenerated at the end of each cycle.
